description: Everything relates to itself — even with two variables.
goal: (forall x (forall y (-> (App (App R x) y) (App (App R x) y))))

guides.text: So far all our [predicate|predicates] have taken a single argument: $P(x)$. But [predicate|predicates] can express **relations** between things too. A binary predicate $R(x, y)$ is written `(App (App R x) y)` — nested `App`. This goal says: for all $x$ and $y$, if $R(x,y)$ then $R(x,y)$. The two-variable version of identity.
Same strategy as problem 34, but with two variables. First, introduce a fresh [witness|witness] $x$ with `fix` — 'take any $x$.'
Now introduce a second fresh [witness|witness] $y$ — 'take any $y$.' We're building a proof that works for arbitrary $x$ and $y$.
The two witnesses live in separate [context|contexts]: $\{x\}$ and $\{y\}$. Merge them with `and-intro` so we have both $x$ and $y$ available.
Now the familiar pattern: assume the [antecedent|antecedent] $R(x, y)$ — written as nested `App`: `(App (App R x) y)`.
Merge the witness context (step 3, containing $x$ and $y$) with the assumption (step 4, containing $R(x,y)$). All three are now in one place.
Extract $R(x, y)$ from the merged context — the goal under our witnesses and assumption.
[discharge|Discharge] $R(x,y)$ to form the [implication|implication] $R(x,y) \to R(x,y)$. Context: $\{x, y\}$.
Generalise over $y$ with `forall-intro`. Since $y$ is fresh in the remaining context ($\{x\}$), the generalisation is sound. We now have $\forall y.\; R(x,y) \to R(x,y)$.
Generalise over $x$ to finish: $\forall x.\; \forall y.\; R(x,y) \to R(x,y)$. The proof stands for every possible pair.
